ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for potato chips as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for processed snack foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One small packet of potato chips (approximately 30 g) is a typical single-serve snack portion commonly sold in Singapore convenience stores and fast-food outlets.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Potato chips, plain, salted (SR Legacy ID 19411)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for plain salted potato chips (FDC ID 19411) used as the primary nutrient anchor. All nutrient values are directly from this approved source.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Potato chips, plain, salted
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 56 (medium) for plain salted potato chips is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table (Am J Clin Nutr 2008;87:223S–236S). Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (56 × 14.43 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 8.1, rounded to 7.9. Available carbohydrate calculated as total carbohydrate (52.9 g) minus dietary fibre (4.8 g) = 48.1 g/100g. For a 30g serving, 48.1 * 0.30 = 14.43g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (525 mg/100 g); a 30 g serving delivers approximately 158 mg sodium, representing about 7% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit commercial salted potato chips due to the high sodium load; if consumed, restrict to no more than one small packet (30 g) infrequently and choose unsalted or low-sodium varieties where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (56) combined with high carbohydrate density (52.9 g/100 g) and high fat content (35.4 g/100 g) produces a meaningful glycaemic and caloric burden; deep-frying also slows gastric emptying, creating a prolonged postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit potato chips to very small occasional portions (≤30 g) and pair with a protein or fibre source to blunt the glycaemic response; baked or air-fried lower-carbohydrate alternatives are strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (35.4 g/100 g), saturated fat (9.5 g/100 g), high sodium (525 mg/100 g), and ultra-processed formulation collectively elevate cardiovascular risk; the energy density of 536 kcal/100 g also contributes to weight gain, a major CVD risk factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat potato chips as an occasional treat only, limiting portions to 30 g or less, and prioritise whole-food snacks such as unsalted nuts, fresh fruit, or raw vegetables to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote systemic inflammation; potatoes are a nightshade vegetable that may exacerbate joint symptoms in sensitive individuals; sodium at 525 mg/100 g exceeds the RA caution threshold of 400 mg/100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried potato chips due to AGE formation and the nightshade concern; choose anti-inflammatory snacks such as walnuts, berries, or vegetable sticks instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Potato chips (also called crisps in British English) are thin slices of potato that have been deep-fried in vegetable oil and seasoned with salt. They are one of the most widely consumed ultra-processed snack foods globally, popular in Singapore as a side accompaniment to sandwiches, burgers, and wraps, or eaten on their own as a between-meal snack. The texture is characteristically crispy and crunchy, with a savoury, salty flavour and a rich, oily mouthfeel. Per 100 g, plain salted potato chips provide approximately 536 kcal, 35.4 g fat (of which 9.5 g is saturated), 52.9 g carbohydrate, 4.8 g dietary fibre, 6.56 g protein, and 525 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is approximately 56 (medium), but the high carbohydrate density and fat content make the overall glycaemic and caloric load significant. Clinically, potato chips are a high-concern food for hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high carbohydrate and caloric density), and cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat and sodium). Deep-frying also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory and of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. A standard single-serve packet in Singapore is approximately 30 g. Potato chips are vegetarian, vegan, and typically gluten-free, though cross-contamination risks exist in shared manufacturing facilities.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a packet of chips?
What is the GI of potato chips?
Can I eat chips if I have high blood pressure?
How much fat is in potato chips?
Are crisps and chips the same thing?
What is a healthy alternative to potato chips?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are anchored to USDA SR Legacy entry 19411 (plain salted potato chips), an approved primary source with exact match to the food category. GI of 56 is drawn from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table. All sources are approved and directly support the data.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy 19411 — plain salted potato chips contain no industrially produced trans fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy 19411 — potato chips fried in vegetable oil contain no dietary cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero — plant-derived food with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: not reported in USDA SR Legacy 19411 for this entry. folate_mcg_dfe set to -1: not reported in USDA SR Legacy 19411 for this entry. vitamin_d_mcg set to -1: not reported in USDA SR Legacy 19411 for this entry. Input data source was GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7); all nutrient values have been superseded by USDA SR Legacy 19411 as the approved source. The food is classified as Western cuisine and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under the G6 multilingual rule, which applies to Singaporean dishes. Alias coverage is provided in English only, consistent with the Western cuisine classification.
